chunks.doc_title: Evaluation Metrics for RAG and LLM Systems
chunks.content: Evaluating RAG systems requires measuring both retrieval quality and generation quality separately, since failures can originate at either stage. Retrieval metrics include Precision@k (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant), Recall@k (fraction of all relevant documents that are retrieved in top-k), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG). Generation metrics include faithfulness (whether the answer is supported by retrieved context), answer relevance (whether the answer addresses the question), and context utilization. The RAGAS framework provides automated metrics for faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall using an LLM as a judge. Latency is a key operational metric: retrieval latency, generation latency, and end-to-end latency should all be measured separately to identify bottlenecks.

chunks.doc_title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
chunks.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that enhances large language model outputs by grounding them in external knowledge retrieved at inference time. Unlike purely parametric models that rely solely on weights learned during training, RAG systems query a vector database to fetch relevant context before generating a response. The RAG pipeline consists of two main phases: indexing and querying. During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings using an encoder model such as sentence-transformers, and stored in a vector database like FAISS or ChromaDB. During querying, the user question is also embedded into the same vector space. A similarity search retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks. These chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context, enabling the model to generate factually grounded responses and reducing hallucinations. Key metrics for evaluating RAG systems include retrieval precision@k, recall@k, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance. Hallucination rate measures how often the model generates claims not supported by the retrieved context.

chunks.doc_title: Chunking Strategies for RAG Systems
chunks.content: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding and indexing. The chunking strategy has a significant impact on retrieval quality in RAG systems. Fixed-size chunking splits documents into chunks of a predetermined token or character count such as 512 tokens with optional overlap such as 50 tokens. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries but increases index size. Semantic chunking uses sentence boundary detection or topic modeling to create chunks that represent coherent semantic units regardless of length. This can improve precision but requires more complex preprocessing. Chunk size tradeoffs: smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision by reducing noise, but may lose surrounding context needed for generation. Larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) preserve more context but reduce retrieval precision. A common best practice is to use 512 tokens with 10-15 percent overlap.

chunks.doc_title: LLM Serving Infrastructure and Inference Optimization
chunks.content: Serving large language models efficiently in production requires specialized inference infrastructure that can handle high concurrency, low latency, and large model sizes. vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving framework that uses PagedAttention to manage GPU memory efficiently. It achieves up to 24x higher throughput compared to naive Hugging Face Transformers inference by batching requests dynamically. Ollama is a lightweight tool for running open-weight LLMs locally. It packages models with their runtime dependencies and exposes a simple REST API, making it ideal for development and single-machine deployments. Quantization reduces model size and inference latency by representing weights in lower precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. GGUF format used by llama.cpp enables CPU-based inference for quantized models with minimal quality degradation. Key metrics for LLM serving include time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens per second (throughput), and GPU memory utilization.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that enhances large language model outputs by grounding them in external knowledge retrieved at inference time. The RAG pipeline consists of two main phases: indexing and querying. During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings using an encoder model such as sentence-transformers, and stored in a vector database like FAISS or ChromaDB. The key metrics used to evaluate RAG systems include:
1. Retrieval precision@k (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant)
2. Recall@k (fraction of all relevant documents that are retrieved in top-k)
3. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)
4. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)
5. Answer faithfulness (whether the answer is supported by retrieved context)
6. Answer relevance (whether the answer addresses the question)
7. Context utilization
8. Hallucination rate (how often the model generates claims not supported by the retrieved context).

chunks.doc_title: Vector Databases and Embedding Indexes
chunks.content: Vector databases are specialized storage systems designed to efficiently index and query high-dimensional embedding vectors. Unlike traditional relational databases, vector databases support approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search, which enables fast semantic similarity lookup across millions of documents. FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a widely used open-source library for ANN search. It supports several index types including Flat (exact brute-force), IVF (inverted file index for approximate search), and HNSW (hierarchical navigable small world graphs). ChromaDB is a newer open-source vector database designed specifically for LLM applications. It provides a simple Python API, supports persistent storage, metadata filtering, and integrates natively with LangChain and LlamaIndex. Weaviate, Qdrant, and Milvus are production-grade vector databases with distributed architectures, offering features like multi-tenancy, hybrid search, and real-time index updates.

chunks.doc_title: Feature Stores in Machine Learning
chunks.content: A feature store is a centralized repository for storing, managing, and serving machine learning features. It bridges the gap between data engineering and model training and serving by providing consistent feature definitions across the ML lifecycle. Feature stores have two serving layers: the offline store used for batch training, and the online store used for real-time inference. The offline store is typically a data warehouse like BigQuery or Snowflake. The online store is typically a low-latency key-value store like Redis or DynamoDB. The most critical problem feature stores solve is training-serving skew, the discrepancy between features computed during training and features computed during serving. By using the same feature transformation logic in both contexts, feature stores eliminate this skew. Popular feature store implementations include Feast (open-source), Tecton, Hopsworks, and Vertex AI Feature Store. They support point-in-time correct feature retrieval.

traces.final_answer:
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) and ChromaDB are both open-source vector databases designed for efficient indexing and querying of high-dimensional embedding vectors, but they have some key differences in their focus, features, and use cases.

FAISS is a widely used library for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search, supporting several index types like Flat, IVF, and HNSW. It's primarily focused on providing fast semantic similarity lookup across millions of documents and is not specifically designed for LLM applications. FAISS is a versatile tool that can be used in various domains, such as image recognition, text search, and recommendation systems.

On the other hand, ChromaDB is a newer vector database designed specifically for LLM (Language Model) applications. It provides a simple Python API, supports persistent storage, metadata filtering, and integrates natively with LangChain and LlamaIndex. ChromaDB is optimized for handling text data and is particularly useful in the context of natural language processing tasks like question answering, summarization, and chatbots.

In summary, FAISS should be used when you need a versatile ANN search library for various domains, while ChromaDB is more suitable for LLM applications, especially those involving text data processing in natural language processing tasks.

chunks.doc_title: Model Monitoring and Data Drift Detection
chunks.content: Model monitoring is the practice of continuously observing a deployed machine learning model behavior in production to detect degradation, data drift, and concept drift. Data drift occurs when the statistical distribution of input features in production diverges from the distribution seen during training. Common drift detection methods include the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, Population Stability Index (PSI), and Jensen-Shannon divergence. Concept drift refers to changes in the relationship between input features and the target variable. For example, a fraud detection model may become less effective as fraudsters adapt their strategies over time. Performance monitoring tracks metrics like prediction latency, throughput, and model accuracy. Tools such as Evidently AI, Arize, and WhyLogs provide dashboards for real-time drift detection and alerting. When drift is detected, a retraining trigger is fired initiating an automated pipeline.

chunks.doc_title: MLOps Pipelines and CI/CD for Machine Learning
chunks.content: MLOps applies DevOps principles to machine learning systems, enabling reliable, repeatable, and automated ML workflows in production environments. A typical MLOps pipeline includes data ingestion, data validation, feature engineering, model training, model evaluation, model registration, and model serving. Tools like Apache Airflow, Kubeflow Pipelines, and Prefect are commonly used for orchestration. Continuous Integration for ML involves automatically running data validation checks, unit tests for preprocessing code, and training pipeline smoke tests on each code commit. Continuous Deployment automates the promotion of models from staging to production after passing defined quality gates such as accuracy thresholds or latency benchmarks. Model versioning is a critical MLOps practice. Tools like MLflow, DVC, and Weights and Biases track experiment parameters, metrics, and artifact versions, enabling reproducibility and rollback to previous model versions when production issues arise.

Concept drift refers to changes in the relationship between input features and the target variable. In production Machine Learning (ML) systems, data drift is detected by continuously monitoring the statistical distribution of input features compared to the distribution seen during training. This process is known as model monitoring. Common methods for detecting data drift include the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, Population Stability Index (PSI), and Jensen-Shannon divergence.

When data drift is detected, it indicates that the input features in production have changed from what was used during training, potentially causing the model to degrade in performance. chunks.doc_title: Chunking Strategies for RAG Systems
chunks.content: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding and indexing. The chunking strategy has a significant impact on retrieval quality in RAG systems. Fixed-size chunking splits documents into chunks of a predetermined token or character count such as 512 tokens with optional overlap such as 50 tokens. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries but increases index size. Semantic chunking uses sentence boundary detection or topic modeling to create chunks that represent coherent semantic units regardless of length. This can improve precision but requires more complex preprocessing. Chunk size tradeoffs: smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision by reducing noise, but may lose surrounding context needed for generation. Larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) preserve more context but reduce retrieval precision. A common best practice is to use 512 tokens with 10-15 percent overlap.

chunks.doc_title: Evaluation Metrics for RAG and LLM Systems
chunks.content: Evaluating RAG systems requires measuring both retrieval quality and generation quality separately, since failures can originate at either stage. Retrieval metrics include Precision@k (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant), Recall@k (fraction of all relevant documents that are retrieved in top-k), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG). Generation metrics include faithfulness (whether the answer is supported by retrieved context), answer relevance (whether the answer addresses the question), and context utilization. The RAGAS framework provides automated metrics for faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall using an LLM as a judge. Latency is a key operational metric: retrieval latency, generation latency, and end-to-end latency should all be measured separately to identify bottlenecks.

chunks.doc_title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
chunks.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that enhances large language model outputs by grounding them in external knowledge retrieved at inference time. Unlike purely parametric models that rely solely on weights learned during training, RAG systems query a vector database to fetch relevant context before generating a response. The RAG pipeline consists of two main phases: indexing and querying. During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings using an encoder model such as sentence-transformers, and stored in a vector database like FAISS or ChromaDB. During querying, the user question is also embedded into the same vector space. A similarity search retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks. These chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context, enabling the model to generate factually grounded responses and reducing hallucinations. Key metrics for evaluating RAG systems include retrieval precision@k, recall@k, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance. Hallucination rate measures how often the model generates claims not supported by the retrieved context.

The chunking strategies for RAG systems include fixed-size chunking and semantic chunking.

In fixed-size chunking, documents are split into chunks of a predetermined token or character count, such as 512 tokens with optional overlap like 50 tokens. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries but increases index size.

Semantic chunking uses sentence boundary detection or topic modeling to create chunks that represent coherent semantic units regardless of length. This can improve precision but requires more complex preprocessing.

The tradeoffs for these strategies are as follows:
- Smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision by reducing noise, but may lose surrounding context needed for generation.
- Larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) preserve more context but reduce retrieval precision. A common best practice is to use 512 tokens with 10-15 percent overlap.

chunks.doc_title: LLM Serving Infrastructure and Inference Optimization
chunks.content: Serving large language models efficiently in production requires specialized inference infrastructure that can handle high concurrency, low latency, and large model sizes. vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving framework that uses PagedAttention to manage GPU memory efficiently. It achieves up to 24x higher throughput compared to naive Hugging Face Transformers inference by batching requests dynamically. Ollama is a lightweight tool for running open-weight LLMs locally. It packages models with their runtime dependencies and exposes a simple REST API, making it ideal for development and single-machine deployments. Quantization reduces model size and inference latency by representing weights in lower precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. GGUF format used by llama.cpp enables CPU-based inference for quantized models with minimal quality degradation. Key metrics for LLM serving include time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens per second (throughput), and GPU memory utilization.

The RAG pipeline consists of two main phases: indexing and querying. During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings using an encoder model such as sentence-transformers, and stored in a vector database like FAISS or ChromaDB. During querying, the user question is also embedded into the same vector space. A similarity search retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks. These chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context, enabling the model to generate factually grounded responses and reducing hallucinations. Key metrics for evaluating RAG systems include retrieval precision@k, recall@k, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance. Hallucination rate measures how often the model generates claims not supported by the retrieved context.

chunks.doc_title: Vector Databases and Embedding Indexes
chunks.content: Vector databases are specialized storage systems designed to efficiently index and query high-dimensional embedding vectors. Unlike traditional relational databases, vector databases support approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search, which enables fast semantic similarity lookup across millions of documents. FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a widely used open-source library for ANN search. It supports several index types including Flat (exact brute-force), IVF (inverted file index for approximate search), and HNSW (hierarchical navigable small world graphs). ChromaDB is a newer open-source vector database designed specifically for LLM applications. It provides a simple Python API, supports persistent storage, metadata filtering, and integrates natively with LangChain and LlamaIndex. Weaviate, Qdrant, and Milvus are production-grade vector databases with distributed architectures, offering features like multi-tenancy, hybrid search, and real-time index updates.

chunks.doc_title: Model Monitoring and Data Drift Detection
chunks.content: Model monitoring is the practice of continuously observing a deployed machine learning model behavior in production to detect degradation, data drift, and concept drift. Data drift occurs when the statistical distribution of input features in production diverges from the distribution seen during training. Common drift detection methods include the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, Population Stability Index (PSI), and Jensen-Shannon divergence. Concept drift refers to changes in the relationship between input features and the target variable. For example, a fraud detection model may become less effective as fraudsters adapt their strategies over time. Performance monitoring tracks metrics like prediction latency, throughput, and model accuracy. Tools such as Evidently AI, Arize, and WhyLogs provide dashboards for real-time drift detection and alerting. When drift is detected, a retraining trigger is fired initiating an automated pipeline.

chunks.doc_title: Chunking Strategies for RAG Systems
chunks.content: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding and indexing. The chunking strategy has a significant impact on retrieval quality in RAG systems. Fixed-size chunking splits documents into chunks of a predetermined token or character count such as 512 tokens with optional overlap such as 50 tokens. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries but increases index size. Semantic chunking uses sentence boundary detection or topic modeling to create chunks that represent coherent semantic units regardless of length. This can improve precision but requires more complex preprocessing. Chunk size tradeoffs: smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision by reducing noise, but may lose surrounding context needed for generation. Larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) preserve more context but reduce retrieval precision. A common best practice is to use 512 tokens with 10-15 percent overlap.

A feature store plays a significant role in MLOps by bridging the gap between data engineering and model training and serving. It provides consistent feature definitions across the machine learning (ML) lifecycle, ensuring that the features used during training are the same as those used during serving. This consistency eliminates the problem of training-serving skew, which is a discrepancy between features computed during training and features computed during serving.

The feature store achieves this by using the same feature transformation logic in both contexts. It has two serving layers: an offline store used for batch training, typically a data warehouse like BigQuery or Snowflake; and an online store used for real-time inference, which is usually a low-latency key-value store like Redis or DynamoDB. By using the same feature store for both training and serving, the model can make predictions with features that are consistent with those it was trained on, thus preventing training-serving skew.

In addition to preventing training-serving skew, feature stores also support point-in-time correct feature retrieval, ensuring that the features used in a particular model version are the same as those used when that version was trained. This is crucial for reproducibility and maintaining the integrity of ML models in production environments.

chunks.doc_title: Agentic AI Systems and Tool Use
chunks.content: Agentic AI systems are LLM-powered applications that can autonomously plan, reason, and execute multi-step tasks by calling external tools. Unlike single-turn question answering, agents maintain state across multiple reasoning steps. The ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) framework interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool invocations. At each step, the agent generates a thought, selects an action (tool call), observes the result, and iterates until the task is complete. Common agent tools include web search, code execution, database queries, API calls, and retrieval from vector stores. Tool selection logic can be implemented as rule-based routing, LLM-based function calling, or trained classifiers. Key challenges in agentic systems include error recovery, context length management across long reasoning chains, and preventing infinite loops. Transparency and observability of agent decisions are critical for debugging and evaluation.

chunks.doc_title: LLM Serving Infrastructure and Inference Optimization
chunks.content: Serving large language models efficiently in production requires specialized inference infrastructure that can handle high concurrency, low latency, and large model sizes. vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving framework that uses PagedAttention to manage GPU memory efficiently. It achieves up to 24x higher throughput compared to naive Hugging Face Transformers inference by batching requests dynamically. Ollama is a lightweight tool for running open-weight LLMs locally. It packages models with their runtime dependencies and exposes a simple REST API, making it ideal for development and single-machine deployments. Quantization reduces model size and inference latency by representing weights in lower precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. GGUF format used by llama.cpp enables CPU-based inference for quantized models with minimal quality degradation. Key metrics for LLM serving include time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens per second (throughput), and GPU memory utilization.

chunks.doc_title: Evaluation Metrics for RAG and LLM Systems
chunks.content: Evaluating RAG systems requires measuring both retrieval quality and generation quality separately, since failures can originate at either stage. Retrieval metrics include Precision@k (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant), Recall@k (fraction of all relevant documents that are retrieved in top-k), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG). Generation metrics include faithfulness (whether the answer is supported by retrieved context), answer relevance (whether the answer addresses the question), and context utilization. The RAGAS framework provides automated metrics for faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall using an LLM as a judge. Latency is a key operational metric: retrieval latency, generation latency, and end-to-end latency should all be measured separately to identify bottlenecks.

chunks.content: MLOps applies DevOps principles to machine learning systems, enabling reliable, repeatable, and automated ML workflows in production environments. A typical MLOps pipeline includes data ingestion, data validation, feature engineering, model training, model evaluation, model registration, and model serving. Tools like Apache Airflow, Kubeflow Pipelines, and Prefect are commonly used for orchestration. Continuous Integration for ML involves automatically running data validation checks, unit tests for preprocessing code, and training pipeline smoke tests on each code commit. Continuous Deployment automates the promotion of models from staging to production after passing defined quality gates such as accuracy thresholds or latency benchmarks. Model versioning is a critical MLOps practice. Tools like MLflow, DVC, and Weights and Biases track experiment parameters, metrics, and artifact versions, enabling reproducibility and rollback to previous model versions when production issues arise.

chunks.doc_title: Embedding Models for Semantic Search
chunks.content: Embedding models convert text into dense vector representations that capture semantic meaning. Similar texts are mapped to nearby points in the embedding space, enabling semantic similarity search. The sentence-transformers library provides a wide range of pre-trained embedding models. Popular models include all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dimensions, fast and lightweight), all-mpnet-base-v2 (768 dimensions, higher quality), and multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1 (optimized for question-answering retrieval). OpenAI text-embedding models are proprietary and require API access. For local and open-source deployments, BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 and E5-large-v2 are strong alternatives. Embedding quality is measured on benchmarks like MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark), which evaluates models across retrieval, clustering, classification, and semantic textual similarity tasks.

chunks.doc_title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
chunks.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that enhances large language model outputs by grounding them in external knowledge retrieved at inference time. Unlike purely parametric models that rely solely on weights learned during training, RAG systems query a vector database to fetch relevant context before generating a response. The RAG pipeline consists of two main phases: indexing and querying. During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings using an encoder model such as sentence-transformers, and stored in a vector database like FAISS or ChromaDB. During querying, the user question is also embedded into the same vector space. A similarity search retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks. These chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context, enabling the model to generate factually grounded responses and reducing hallucinations. Key metrics for evaluating RAG systems include retrieval precision@k, recall@k, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance. Hallucination rate measures how often the model generates claims not supported by the retrieved context.

chunks.doc_title: Chunking Strategies for RAG Systems
chunks.content: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding and indexing. The chunking strategy has a significant impact on retrieval quality in RAG systems. Fixed-size chunking splits documents into chunks of a predetermined token or character count such as 512 tokens with optional overlap such as 50 tokens. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries but increases index size. Semantic chunking uses sentence boundary detection or topic modeling to create chunks that represent coherent semantic units regardless of length. This can improve precision but requires more complex preprocessing. Chunk size tradeoffs: smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision by reducing noise, but may lose surrounding context needed for generation. Larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) preserve more context but reduce retrieval precision. A common best practice is to use 512 tokens with 10-15 percent overlap.

1. **Data Preparation**: Collect and curate a large dataset of documents that will serve as the knowledge base for your RAG system. The data should be relevant to the questions your system is designed to answer.

4. **Querying**: Implement a mechanism to embed user questions into the same vector space as the indexed documents. Perform a similarity search in the vector database to retrieve the top-k most relevant chunks of information.

chunks.doc_title: Model Monitoring and Data Drift Detection
chunks.content: Model monitoring is the practice of continuously observing a deployed machine learning model behavior in production to detect degradation, data drift, and concept drift. Data drift occurs when the statistical distribution of input features in production diverges from the distribution seen during training. Common drift detection methods include the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, Population Stability Index (PSI), and Jensen-Shannon divergence. Concept drift refers to changes in the relationship between input features and the target variable. For example, a fraud detection model may become less effective as fraudsters adapt their strategies over time. Performance monitoring tracks metrics like prediction latency, throughput, and model accuracy. Tools such as Evidently AI, Arize, and WhyLogs provide dashboards for real-time drift detection and alerting. When drift is detected, a retraining trigger is fired initiating an automated pipeline.

chunks.doc_title: LLM Serving Infrastructure and Inference Optimization
chunks.content: Serving large language models efficiently in production requires specialized inference infrastructure that can handle high concurrency, low latency, and large model sizes. vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving framework that uses PagedAttention to manage GPU memory efficiently. It achieves up to 24x higher throughput compared to naive Hugging Face Transformers inference by batching requests dynamically. Ollama is a lightweight tool for running open-weight LLMs locally. It packages models with their runtime dependencies and exposes a simple REST API, making it ideal for development and single-machine deployments. Quantization reduces model size and inference latency by representing weights in lower precision formats such as INT8 or INT4. GGUF format used by llama.cpp enables CPU-based inference for quantized models with minimal quality degradation. Key metrics for LLM serving include time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens per second (throughput), and GPU memory utilization.
